title: Configure Listener Rules for API Gateway
content:
For API requests from the client (e.g., /api/accounts) to be sent to the API Gateway backend instead of the Frontend Nginx, we configure a Listener Rule for routing.
Steps to configure: Open the Load Balancers administration page -> Click on your pg-alb name. Select the Listeners and rules tab in the middle of the screen. Click directly on HTTP:80 (the blue link). Select the Rules tab -> click the Add rules button. Set parameters for the new Rule: Rule name: Enter api-rule. Conditions: Click Add condition -> select the type as Path -> enter the path as /api/* -> click Next. Actions: Click Forward to target groups -> select Target Group tg-backend -> click Next. Priority: Enter the number 10. Click the Add rule button to save.

title: Initialize Database via EC2 Bastion Host
content:
Because the RDS database is locked in Private Subnets, personal computers at home cannot connect directly to load the schema. We will set up an EC2 Bastion Host as a temporary bridge in the Public network.
Step 1: Launch EC2 Bastion Host Search for and select the EC2 service -> select Instances -> click the Launch instances button. Setup parameters: Name: Enter pg-bastion. OS: Select Amazon Linux 2023 (Free Tier eligible version). Instance type: Select t2.micro (or t3.micro). Key pair: Select Proceed without a key pair (we will connect directly via the web browser). Network settings: Click Edit: VPC: Select pg-vpc. Subnet: Select the first public subnet (E.g., pg-subnet-public1-ap-southeast-1a). Auto-assign public IP: Select Enable. Security group: Select Create security group -> Name it: pg-bastion-sg -> Inbound rule section: Must select Source as 0.0.0.0/0 (Anywhere IPv4) for SSH port 22 to use the EC2 Instance Connect feature on the browser. Click the Launch instance button. Step 2: Allow Bastion Host to access RDS Go to Security Group pg-ecs-sg -> click Edit inbound rules -> Click Add rule: Type: Select PostgreSQL (port 5432). Source: Select Custom -> select the pg-bastion-sg Security Group of the Bastion Host just created. Click Save rules. Step 3: Login and run SQL commands via browser At the EC2 Instances page, select the pg-bastion virtual machine -> click the Connect button at the top corner. Select the EC2 Instance Connect tab -> click the Connect button. A black Linux terminal screen will display directly on your browser! Run the following commands in the terminal to install the postgresql client and load data: # 1. Install psql client sudo dnf install postgresql15 -y # 2. Create file containing initialization SQL commands cat << 'EOF' > init.sql CREATE DATABASE paymentservice; CREATE DATABASE accountservice; CREATE DATABASE transactionservice; EOF # 3. Execute the command to load the database to RDS (replace <rds-endpoint> with your RDS Endpoint address) # When the system asks for Password, enter: yourpassword (when typing the password will be hidden, just type correctly and press Enter) psql -h <rds-endpoint> -U postgres -d postgres -f init.sql A successful load will report CREATE DATABASE for 3 databases.
Terminate EC2 Bastion Host after finishing loading: Go back to the EC2 Instances management page -> Select virtual machine pg-bastion -> Select Instance state -> select Terminate instance to delete the virtual machine to avoid incurring costs.

title: Launch ECS Services
content:
In this section, we will create an ECS Cluster and launch ECS Services in the Private Subnets.
Step 1: Create ECS Cluster Select Clusters in the left menu -> click Create cluster. Cluster name: Enter pg-cluster. Infrastructure: Select Fargate only (serverless). Click Create. Step 2: Launch Backend Service (pg-backend-service) Go to Cluster pg-cluster -> Services tab -> select Create. Configuration: Task definition family: Select pg-backend (latest revision). Service name: Enter pg-backend-service. Task definition revision: Enter 1. Deployment configuration: Health check grace period: Enter 300. Networking: VPC: Select pg-vpc. Subnets: Check only your 2 Private Subnets (Example: pg-subnet-private1... and pg-subnet-private2...). Please uncheck the Public Subnets. Security group: Select Use existing Security group -> select pg-ecs-sg (remove the default group default). Public IP: Ensure Turned off is selected. Load balancing: Load balancer type: Select Application Load Balancer. Load balancer: Select pg-alb. Container to load balance: Select the container backend:8080:8080. Listener: Select Use an existing listener -> select HTTP:80. Target group: Select Use an existing target group -> select tg-backend. Service auto scaling: Minimum number of tasks: 1. Maximum number of tasks: 5. Scaling policy type: Select Target tracking. Policy name : Enter cpu-auto-scaling. ECS service metric: Select ECSServiceAverageCPUUtilization. Target value: Enter 70. Scale-out cooldown period: Enter 60. Scale-in cooldown period: Enter 300. Turn off scale-in: Uncheck Click Create. Step 3: Launch Frontend Service (pg-frontend-service) Return to the Cluster -> click Create for the second service. Configuration: Task definition family: pg-frontend | Service name: pg-frontend-service | Task definition revision: 1. Networking: VPC pg-vpc, select only 2 Private Subnets, Security Group pg-ecs-sg, Public IP: Turned off. Load balancing: Load balancer: Select pg-alb. Container: Select the container frontend:80:80. Listener: Select Use an existing listener -> select HTTP:80. Target group: Select Use an existing target group -> select tg-frontend. Click Create. Wait for about 1-2 minutes, the status of both Services will change to Running (green color). Now you can use the ALB’s DNS to access the web interface!

title: Set up VPC Networking
content:
In this section, we will set up the VPC Networking infrastructure using the “VPC and more” feature to quickly create 4 Subnets (2 Public Subnets for Load Balancer and 2 Private Subnets for ECS Tasks/RDS Database), along with Internet Gateway and NAT Gateway.
Steps to implement: Initialize VPC with “VPC and more” Configure Routing for NAT Gateway

title: Simulate Load & Test Alarm
content:
We will simulate sending a large number of requests to trigger the CloudWatch Alarm and receive an Email notification.
Step 1: Trigger load simulation Run the PowerShell Script from your personal computer (replace the DNS address of your ALB):
k6 run -e VUS=100 -e ITERATIONS=10000 -e ACCOUNT_IDS="<YOUR_ACCOUNT_UUID>" -e PAYMENT_BASE_URL="http://<YOUR_ALB_URL>" -e ACCOUNT_BASE_URL="http://<YOUR_ALB_URL>" payment_system_k6.js Note: You can also use other benchmark tools to send requests. Step 2: Test the results Wait for about 1-2 minutes. Check the CloudWatch Alarm pg-alb-high-request-alarm: Status will turn red indicating In alarm. Open your email inbox: You will receive an automated alert email from AWS SNS.

title: Take Snapshot & Export to Amazon S3
content:
We proceed to take an RDS Snapshot of pg-db and export that data to S3.
Implementation steps: Take a manual RDS Snapshot: Go to the RDS Console -> Databases -> Select pg-db. Click Actions -> Select Take snapshot -> Name it: pg-db-snapshot-manual -> Click Take snapshot. Wait for the snapshot status to change to Available (green). Export the Snapshot to S3: Go to RDS -> select Snapshots in the left menu -> select the Manual tab -> check the pg-db-snapshot-manual snapshot. Click Actions -> Select Export to Amazon S3. Configure parameters: Export identifier: Enter pg-db-export-to-s3. S3 bucket: Select your correct bucket: pg-db-backups-<account-id>-ap-southeast-1-an. IAM role: Check Choose an existing role -> select the correct role rds-s3-export-role created in the previous step. Encryption (KMS key): Select the correct key pg-s3-export-key created in the previous step. Click Export to Amazon S3 to complete. The Export process will run in the background as compressed Parquet, stored securely in the S3 Bucket.

title: Translated Blogs
content:
Blog 1 - Automating Container Application Deployment to Amazon ECS Express Mode using GitHub Actions This article guides you on how to build a fully automated and secure CI/CD pipeline from GitHub source code to a live environment on AWS. You will learn about core advantages such as passwordless authentication via OIDC, infrastructure automation (ALB, Auto Scaling) thanks to ECS Express Mode, and precise version management using Commit SHA to optimize the DevOps process. Additionally, the article shares practical considerations regarding tightening IAM Policy permissions and how to monitor workflow logs in real-time to detect conflict errors early.
Blog 2 - Optimizing Disaster Recovery for Stateful Services on Amazon EKS using Velero This blog shares a comprehensive disaster recovery backup and restore solution for stateful applications running on an Amazon EKS cluster using the open-source tool Velero. The article delves into the parallel processing architecture (backing up logic configurations to Amazon S3 and taking physical disk EBS Snapshots), the secure authentication mechanism with EKS Pod Identity, and the flexible cross-namespace recovery feature. Besides, the author also summarizes experiences in handling syntax errors in the Windows CMD environment and fixing the situation where Pods are stuck in the Pending state during scheduling.
Blog 3 - Breaking the 90-day Limit of EC2 Capacity Manager with Amazon Athena This article focuses on the problem of optimizing large-scale infrastructure costs (FinOps) on AWS by overcoming the 90-day historical data storage limit of EC2 Capacity Manager. You will understand the process of automatically packing data into Parquet format for long-term storage on Amazon S3 and optimizing queries using the Partition Projection mechanism on Amazon Athena without manual scanning. In particular, the article provides 3 practical SQL scenarios to help enterprises easily hunt down wasted ODCR resource packages, identify peak load patterns, and proactively share internal unused capacity.

title: Set up Security Groups
content:
In this section, we will create Security Groups to act as a virtual firewall controlling inbound/outbound traffic to the Application Load Balancer, ECS Tasks, and RDS Database.
Step 4.1: Create Security Group for Application Load Balancer (pg-alb-sg) The ALB will be the checkpoint receiving direct traffic from the Internet sent to the system:
Open the EC2 service on the AWS Console -> Scroll down the left menu to the Network & Security section -> click on Security Groups. Click the Create security group button on the right side of the screen. Setup information: Security group name: Enter pg-alb-sg. Description: Enter Allow HTTP traffic from internet. VPC: Click select pg-vpc (Absolutely do not select the default VPC). Configure Inbound rules: Click Add rule: Type: Select HTTP (port 80). Source: Select Anywhere-IPv4 (0.0.0.0/0) to allow access from everyone. Click the Create security group button at the bottom. Step 4.2: Create Security Group for Containers & Database (pg-ecs-sg) This Security Group will protect the Fargate Tasks running Frontend, combined Backend, and RDS PostgreSQL database in the Private network:
Go back to the Security Groups page -> Click the Create security group button. Setup information: Security group name: Enter pg-ecs-sg. Description: Enter Allow traffic from ALB and internal containers. VPC: Click select pg-vpc. Configure Inbound rules: Add the following 4 specific rules to ensure strict authorization: Rule 1 (For Frontend): Type: HTTP | Source: Select Custom -> select the Security Group pg-alb-sg just created in Step 4.1. Rule 2 (For Spring Boot Backend): Type: Select Custom TCP | Port range: Enter 8080 | Source: Select Custom -> select the Security Group pg-alb-sg. Click the Create security group button at the bottom. Go to Security Group pg-ecs-sg -> click Edit inbound rules -> Click Add rule: Rule 3 (For PostgreSQL Database): Type: Select PostgreSQL (port 5432) | Source: Select Custom -> select this very Security Group pg-ecs-sg being created. (This method allows the Backend container associated with the SG to have permission to call RDS). Rule 4 (Internal connection): Type: Select All traffic | Source: Select Custom -> select this very Security Group pg-ecs-sg being created. (Helps the Java container cross-connect with the Redis container sidecar on the same task).Click Save rules.

title: Workshop
content:
Deploying High-Concurrency Payment Gateway on AWS ECS Fargate with Private Subnets, RDS PostgreSQL, and S3 Backup Overview In this workshop, we will manually build and deploy a complete, secure, and highly available cloud infrastructure on AWS for a High-Concurrency Payment Gateway application.
The system will be designed according to enterprise security standards, including Public/Private Subnets segregation, using an Application Load Balancer (ALB) to receive and route traffic, running backend microservices bundled with a Redis sidecar on AWS ECS Fargate, connecting to an internal Amazon RDS PostgreSQL database, and setting up an S3 Backup & Recovery mechanism via the Snapshot Export feature integrated with a KMS Key.
Practice content Workshop Overview Environment Preparation & AWS ECR VPC Networking Setup Security Groups Setup Initialize RDS PostgreSQL in Private Network Application Load Balancer Configuration Deploy ECS Fargate Configure CloudWatch Alarm & SNS Notification Integrate S3 Backup & Recovery Verification & Acceptance Resource Cleanup

title: Self Evaluation
content:
During my 12-week internship in the First Cloud Journey (FCJ) program, I had the opportunity to learn, practice, and apply a vast amount of cloud computing and DevOps knowledge to practical projects. This process not only helped me transition from an on-premise mindset to a cloud-native approach but also trained me to work professionally, handle pressure, and solve real-world enterprise problems.
I successfully designed and deployed a High-Concurrency Payment Gateway architecture, implementing a secure 3-tier VPC network, containerization with Docker & AWS ECS Fargate, an isolated RDS PostgreSQL database, and setting up CloudWatch/SNS monitoring combined with encrypted backups using S3 & KMS. Furthermore, I deeply researched high-concurrency architectures such as In-memory Token Bucket Rate Limiting, Distributed Lock, and Idempotency filters using Redis. I also attended community events to hone my skills in AI, Prompt Engineering, and DevOps career paths. Through this, I significantly improved my skills in: Cloud System Design, Containerization, Serverless Operations, Network Security, FinOps, and Practical Product Mindset.
Regarding my working attitude, I strictly maintained discipline by following the 12-week Worklog schedule, proactively updating myself on new tech trends, translating in-depth technical blogs, and continuously testing and debugging to complete the Workshop.
To objectively reflect on my internship journey, I would like to self-evaluate based on the criteria below:
No. Criteria Description Good Fair Average 1 Professional Knowledge & Skills Mastered the AWS ecosystem, successfully deploying ECS Fargate container and serverless infra ☐ ✅ ☐ 2 Learning Ability Quickly grasped new technologies from tech events (GraphRAG, Serverless WebSocket, Rate Limiting) ✅ ☐ ☐ 3 Proactiveness Independently researched, built labs, and actively experimented with Fargate and k6 load testing ☐ ✅ ☐ 4 Sense of Responsibility Strictly adhered to the 12-week roadmap, completing the Worklog, Proposal, and Workshop on time ☐ ✅ ☐ 5 Discipline Despite occasional procrastination, strictly followed security protocols and Free Tier limits ☐ ☐ ✅ 6 Drive for Improvement Willing to tackle high-concurrency challenges, upgrading system architecture week by week ✅ ☐ ☐ 7 Communication Needs to reduce technical jargon when explaining AWS architectures to beginners ☐ ☐ ✅ 8 Teamwork Worked mostly independently; needs to proactively seek team projects and practice Agile/Git ☐ ☐ ✅ 9 Professional Conduct Actively participated in FCJ Community Day events and respected community guidelines ☐ ✅ ☐ 10 Problem-Solving Mindset Applied Root Cause Analysis to debug database overloads and optimize caching performance (Redis) ☐ ✅ ☐ 11 Contribution to Organization Delivered a high-quality AWS Workshop serving as a comprehensive reference for future members ☐ ✅ ☐ 12 Overall Assessment Successfully overcame old mindsets, building a solid foundation toward becoming a DevOps Engineer ✅ ☐ ☐ Areas for Improvement Although I have made significant progress, I acknowledge some realistic weaknesses that require serious attention and improvement:
Time Management and Discipline (Rating: Average): Occasionally, I suffered from procrastination during the early weeks, which led to a pile-up of tasks over the weekends. I need to strictly apply the 5-Minute Rule and the Pomodoro technique to optimize daily productivity and avoid last-minute rushes. Communication and Presentation Skills (Rating: Average): When writing reports or explaining AWS workflows to beginners, I sometimes overused technical jargon, making it difficult for the audience to follow. I need to practice expressing complex ideas simply, using storytelling and visual diagrams instead of plain text. Team Collaboration and Agile/Scrum (Rating: Average): I spent the majority of the project researching and working independently. Therefore, I lacked hands-on experience in standard team collaboration workflows (e.g., Git branching, creating Pull Requests, peer code reviews, or resolving merge conflicts). I must proactively join larger team projects to develop this essential survival skill. Expanding Infrastructure as Code (IaC) Skills: Despite understanding system flows, my deployments were primarily manual (click-ops) via the AWS Console. This approach is highly prone to human errors and hard to replicate. My mandatory next goal is to translate this entire architecture into code using Terraform or AWS CloudFormation. Advanced Technical English: Although I can look up technical documentation, my reading speed and conversational reflexes in English meetings are still quite slow. This will be a major barrier when pitching ideas to international clients or working in multinational corporations.

title: Feedback & Suggestions
content:
Below are my sincere feedback and reflections after dedicating 12 weeks to the First Cloud AI Journey (FCAJ) program. Completing the High-Concurrency Payment Gateway project, the extensive Workshops, and the technical Blogs has left me with invaluable experiences.
Overall Assessment 1. Working and Learning Environment
The FCAJ community environment is extremely practical and hands-on. Unlike traditional theoretical courses, manually configuring everything from VPCs, EC2s, ALBs to ECS Fargate and RDS made me truly feel the pressure and responsibility of a real Cloud Engineer. The Q&A spaces on Discord/Slack were always active, helping to quickly resolve difficult bugs (like server crashes during k6 load testing).
2. Support from Mentors / Organizers
I was deeply impressed by the mentors’ philosophy of “Guiding the way, not doing the work.” When I encountered an IAM Policy permission error for the S3 KMS bucket, the mentor did not send the solution JSON file but instead provided documentation and keywords for me to debug it myself. This was frustrating at first but turned out to be the best way to train my Root Cause Analysis mindset.
3. Alignment with Enterprise Realities
The program aligns 100% with the needs of modern enterprises. Knowledge such as deploying Serverless architectures (Lambda, API Gateway), cost optimization (FinOps), and especially high-concurrency systems using Redis (Rate Limiting, Idempotency) are incredibly valuable “weapons” rarely taught in depth at universities. The sheer volume of knowledge completely exceeded my initial expectations.
4. Opportunities for Learning & Skill Development
Besides AWS configuration skills, I also honed many “soft skills for tech folks.” This included translating in-depth technical blogs about GraphRAG and NIDS, writing clear budget Proposals, and structuring Workshop documentation so future learners can easily follow. The FCJ Community Day events (Events 1, 2, 3) also strongly inspired me regarding the DevOps career path and how to overcome psychological procrastination.
5. Culture & Builder Spirit
The “Go Build” and “Fail Fast, Learn Faster” mentality was deeply ingrained throughout the 12 weeks. Everyone shared a spirit of not fearing mistakes, ready to tear down and rebuild systems when they crashed. Knowledge sharing was highly valued, proven by the fact that everyone published their Worklogs to learn from one another.
Additional Questions What were you most satisfied with?
The shift in mindset. I no longer write code based on feelings (“vibe code”); instead, I think according to architectural standards, considering security (Private Subnets), performance (Caching), and costs before actually building. What do you think the community needs to improve?
Although the guide is very detailed, configuring CLI or handling permission errors can be a bit challenging for beginners who are new to AWS. The community could add more troubleshooting guides for common issues and provide deeper reference materials to help interns easily overcome initial technical hurdles. Would you recommend this to a friend?
100% absolutely. This is not a superficial “walk in the park” program. Anyone who truly wants to endure rigorous training to mature and build an extremely heavy-weight portfolio (Workshop + Proposal) for job hunting must join FCJ. Proposals & Desires Suggestions for improvement: Organize regular short sharing sessions (Showcases or small group discussions). This would be an excellent opportunity for interns to exchange ways to resolve deployment issues, share debugging tips, and learn from each other to improve their skills together. Future direction: I plan to continue self-study and upgrade my current system to a higher level (such as experimenting with infrastructure automation). At the same time, I will strive to hone my remaining technical skills and accumulate more practical experience to prepare for upcoming career opportunities in the Cloud and DevOps fields.

title: Verification & Results
content:
After deploying all infrastructure components (VPC, RDS, ALB, ECS Fargate, CloudWatch Alarm, S3 Backup), this is the final step to test and verify the entire system’s correctness and performance.
Demo Video: To visually inspect the entire web interface interaction and simulated k6 load testing execution on AWS, please refer to the System Demo & Load Testing Video.
Step 10.1: Verify Target Group Health Status Before accessing the web UI, we must ensure that the Application Load Balancer has successfully registered and established connectivity to the Fargate Tasks running in the Private Subnets:
Go to the EC2 service -> select Target Groups from the left navigation pane. Verify the health status of both Target Groups: tg-frontend: Under the Targets tab, the IP address list of the Frontend Task must show a Healthy status on port 80. tg-backend: The IP address list of the Backend Task must show a Healthy status on port 8080. Step 10.2: Access and Test the Web Interface Go to the EC2 service -> click Load Balancers -> select pg-alb. Copy the DNS name Paste this DNS address into your web browser to access the High-Concurrency Payment Gateway application: Step 10.3: Inspect Container Logs via CloudWatch To monitor internal microservice activities and verify container-to-container communication:
Go to the CloudWatch console. Select Log groups from the left pane -> click on the /ecs/pg-logs log group. Check the Log Streams corresponding to each container: backend/...: Contains logs from the Spring Boot services (API Gateway, Account Service, Payment Service, Transaction Service). Look for connection confirmation messages pointing to the database host and the Redis sidecar (127.0.0.1:6379). redis/...: Contains execution logs of the Redis cache engine running alongside the Java container. frontend/...: Shows access and error logs for the Nginx proxy serving static assets. Step 10.4: Check Request Metrics in CloudWatch We can view real-time traffic statistics arriving at our Load Balancer:
In the CloudWatch console, go to Metrics -> All metrics in the left menu. Search and select the RequestCount metric belonging to your Load Balancer pg-alb (or go to the Graphed metrics tab to adjust the time window). Select Sum as the Statistic and 5 minutes (or 1 minute) as the Period. You should see the traffic visualization graph: The graph will plot a sharp spike (e.g., reaching over 5500 requests) representing the load test script execution you ran via PowerShell. Step 10.5: Load Testing & Auto Scaling Results Assessment After executing a high-concurrency load test using k6, we will review the achieved metrics to evaluate the robustness of our Microservices architecture on AWS.
High Concurrency Handling:
Based on the load test summary, the system successfully withstood an extreme scenario: 100 virtual users (VUs) continuously generating 10,000 payment transactions. Processing Speed: The entire process of checking balances, deducting funds, and saving transaction history across 3 microservices was completed fully in just 1 minute and 50 seconds. The success rate reached 99.78%. Only a tiny fraction (0.22%) of requests were actively blocked by the API Gateway and Redis Rate Limiter to protect the server from a global crash (Overload Protection). Absolute Data Integrity (Data Invariant & Distributed Lock):
Observe the PAYMENT INVARIANT CHECK section in the output: Even with thousands of transactions concurrently attempting to deduct money from the same account, the final balance in the database matched perfectly (Error margin delta = 0.00). This result confirms that the Redis Distributed Lock mechanism and Idempotency functioned flawlessly, preventing any data loss or inconsistencies (Race Conditions). Monitoring and Auto Scaling (CloudWatch & Target Tracking): From the CloudWatch Alarms console, our configured sensors acted highly responsively: AlarmLow (CPU < 63%): Transitioned to the In alarm state precisely when traffic subsided, triggering a scale-in to save costs. AlarmHigh (CPU > 70%): Closely monitored. Thanks to the exceptional processing capability of the Fargate task (1 vCPU, 2GB RAM), the system consumed all 10,000 requests before reaching the time threshold required to trigger a scale-out. Conclusion: The High-Concurrency Payment Gateway system is now completely production-ready, successfully meeting the strictest standards for Reliability, Scalability, and Data Consistency.

title: Resource Cleanup
content:
To avoid incurring unexpected charges on your AWS account after completing the lab, please proceed to clean up and delete all initialized resources in the exact order instructed below.
Step 11.1: Delete ECS Services & Clusters Go to the ECS service -> click the pg-cluster Cluster. Select the Services tab: Check the pg-frontend-service service -> Click the Delete button -> Enter the confirmation text to delete. Check the pg-backend-service service -> Click the Delete button -> Confirm deletion. Wait for the Tasks to stop completely. Click the Delete cluster button in the upper right corner to delete the Cluster. Step 11.2: Delete Application Load Balancer & Target Groups Go to the EC2 service -> scroll down to the Load Balancing section: Click Load Balancers -> Check pg-alb -> Click Actions -> select Delete load balancer -> Confirm deletion. Click Target Groups in the left menu -> Check both Target Groups tg-frontend and tg-backend -> Click Actions -> select Delete -> Confirm deletion. Step 11.3: Delete RDS PostgreSQL Database Go to the RDS service -> select Databases in the left menu. Check your pg-db database. Click the Actions button -> select Delete. Configure the deletion page: Uncheck “Create final snapshot?” (Do not take a final snapshot to avoid storage charges). Check “I acknowledge that…” to confirm. Enter the confirmation keyword (usually delete me) in the empty box. Click the Delete button and wait for the database deletion to complete (usually takes 5-10 minutes). Click the Subnet groups section in the left RDS menu -> Select and delete pg-db-subnet-group. Step 11.4: Delete NAT Gateway & Release Elastic IP The NAT Gateway is a resource with a very high hourly charge, so it needs to be deleted immediately:
Go to the VPC service -> select NAT gateways in the left menu. Check the NAT Gateway pg-nat (or the NAT generated by the VPC Wizard). Click Actions -> select Delete NAT gateway -> Confirm deletion. Release Elastic IP: Select the Elastic IPs section in the left VPC menu. Check the static IP previously associated with the NAT gateway. Click Actions -> select Release Elastic IP addresses -> Confirm release. (If you do not release unused static IPs, AWS will still charge your account). Step 11.5: Delete VPC In the VPC service, select Your VPCs in the left menu. Check your pg-vpc VPC. Click Actions -> select Delete VPC. AWS will automatically analyze and clean up all Subnets, Internet Gateways, Route Tables, and security groups associated with this VPC. Click Delete VPC to confirm. Step 11.6: Delete ECR Repositories, S3 Bucket, and KMS Key ECR Repositories: Go to the ECR service -> Check the 2 repositories pg-combined-backend and pg-frontend -> Click Delete to delete the Docker Images. S3 Bucket: Go to the S3 service -> Click the S3 Bucket pg-db-backups-<account-id>-ap-southeast-1-an. Click the Empty button to delete all snapshot files inside -> Confirm. Go back to the Bucket list -> Check that bucket and click the Delete button -> Enter the bucket name to confirm permanent deletion. KMS Key: Go to the KMS service -> click the pg-s3-export-key key -> click Key actions -> select Schedule key deletion -> Select the minimum number of days (7 days) -> click confirm. Step 11.7: Delete CloudWatch Log Groups, Alarms, and SNS Topics CloudWatch Log Group: Go to the CloudWatch service -> select Log groups in the left menu -> Search and check /ecs/pg-logs -> click Actions -> select Delete log group(s) -> Confirm deletion. CloudWatch Alarm: Still in CloudWatch -> select All alarms under Alarms -> check pg-alb-high-request-alarm -> click Actions -> select Delete -> Confirm deletion. SNS Topic: Go to the Simple Notification Service (SNS) service -> select Topics in the left menu -> check pg-alerts -> click Delete -> Confirm deletion (Also clean up any associated email subscriptions under Subscriptions if needed). Step 11.8: Delete IAM Roles and IAM Policies IAM Roles: Go to the IAM service -> select Roles in the left menu: Search and check ecsTaskExecutionRole -> click Delete -> enter role name to confirm deletion. Search and check rds-s3-export-role -> click Delete -> enter role name to confirm deletion. IAM Policy: Select Policies in the left IAM menu -> search for rds-s3-export-policy -> click Actions -> select Delete -> Confirm deletion. Congratulations on successfully and securely completing the lab!
